questions.question: What is the main purpose of contract testing between a provider and its consumers?
options: To catch compatibility regressions before deployment without relying only on production traffic.
To replace all integration and end-to-end testing permanently.
To guarantee every consumer upgrades at the same time.
To avoid documenting ownership boundaries.
questions.explanation: Contract tests provide a focused early warning for contract breakage.

questions.question: Why do ownership boundaries matter for shared contracts?
options: Because someone must own the promise, review changes, and respond when compatibility breaks.
Because ownership removes the need for any CI validation.
Because only public APIs need owners.
Because consumers should never influence contract expectations.
questions.explanation: Clear ownership turns a vague interface into a managed operational commitment.

questions.question: What is the key weakness of relying only on end-to-end tests for contract safety?
options: They usually find breakage later, with noisier diagnosis and more environmental coupling.
They always run faster than focused CI checks.
They cannot observe status codes or payloads.
They eliminate semantic regressions by default.
questions.explanation: End-to-end tests are useful, but they are often too broad and late for sharp contract protection.

questions.question: Which provider change should most clearly trigger contract validation?
options: Any shape or semantic change that could alter consumer expectations.
Only infrastructure changes with no API effect.
A code comment update in an unrelated module.
Rotating an internal build cache.
questions.explanation: Contract gates should focus on changes that affect consumer-visible behavior.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with CI-based contract gating?
options: More setup and fixture maintenance in exchange for earlier, cheaper compatibility detection.
No maintenance cost with complete production realism.
Guaranteed elimination of all integration bugs.
The ability to avoid observability during rollout.
questions.explanation: Contract gates are valuable, but they require discipline and upkeep.

questions.question: How can a contract test still miss a real production issue?
options: If it validates only payload shape and ignores semantic behaviors like defaults, ordering, or status codes.
If it runs in CI instead of production.
If the provider owns the contract documentation.
If consumers publish examples of expected behavior.
questions.explanation: Shape-only testing can miss semantic regressions that matter to consumers.

questions.question: What is the strongest ownership model for a shared internal contract?
options: The provider owns publication and change control, while consumers publish concrete expectations used in verification.
No one owns it because all teams share responsibility equally.
Only consumers own it because providers should move quickly.
Only SRE owns it because availability is the main concern.
questions.explanation: That split creates accountability while keeping consumer expectations visible.

questions.question: When a provider must introduce an intentional breaking change, what is the best next step?
options: Use the contract process to version, stage, communicate, and monitor the migration rather than shipping it silently.
Disable contract tests so rollout is not blocked.
Force all consumers to upgrade instantly without telemetry.
Hide the change behind a renamed environment variable.
questions.explanation: Intentional breaks still need structured rollout and visibility.
